metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: English Language Arts 9 deepens students' skills in reading, writing, speaking, listening, and representing through engagement with increasingly complex texts. Students examine how texts are socially and culturally constructed, how language shapes identity, and how perspectives shape interpretation. There is a growing emphasis on rhetorical awareness, opinion writing with evidence, and understanding the diversity within First Peoples societies represented in texts.
metadata.general_description: An integrated study of reading, writing, speaking, listening, and representing through complex texts, with growing emphasis on rhetorical analysis, evidence-based writing, and critical literacy.
metadata.course_structure_note: ELA 9 is a single integrated course. Content builds directly from ELA 8, adding rhetorical devices, language change, irony, and opinion writing.

big_ideas.statement: Language and story can be a source of creativity and joy.
big_ideas.elaboration: Both reading and creating stories provide pleasure, aesthetic experience, and the creative freedom to explore possibilities beyond the real.

big_ideas.statement: Exploring stories and other texts helps us understand ourselves and make connections to others and to the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: Texts — whether novels, films, podcasts, or oral stories — are mirrors and windows, helping us see ourselves and understand others' experiences.

big_ideas.statement: People understand text differently depending on their worldviews and perspectives.
big_ideas.elaboration: Interpretation is not neutral. A reader's identity, experience, and cultural background all shape what they notice, value, and take from a text.

big_ideas.statement: Texts are socially, culturally, and historically constructed.
big_ideas.elaboration: No text exists in a vacuum — it is shaped by who wrote it, when, where, for whom, and under what social conditions.

big_ideas.statement: Questioning what we hear, read, and view contributes to our ability to be educated and engaged citizens.
big_ideas.elaboration: Critically evaluating information, opinions, and narratives — rather than accepting them passively — is a cornerstone of democratic citizenship.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect (Reading, Listening, Viewing)
categories.raw: Comprehend and Connect — Reading, Listening, Viewing

competencies.raw: Access information and ideas for diverse purposes from a variety of sources and evaluate their relevance, accuracy, and reliability.
i_can_statements: I can access information from a variety of sources for a specific purpose.
I can evaluate whether a source is relevant, accurate, and reliable.
I can compare how different sources present the same information and explain the differences.

competencies.raw: Apply appropriate strategies to comprehend written, oral, and visual texts, guide inquiry, and extend thinking.
i_can_statements: I can select and apply the right comprehension strategy for the type of text I'm reading, viewing, or listening to.
I can use my comprehension strategies to guide my own inquiry into a topic.
I can extend my thinking beyond the literal meaning of a text through inference and analysis.

competencies.raw: Synthesize ideas from a variety of sources to build understanding.
i_can_statements: I can bring together ideas from multiple texts to form a more complex understanding.
I can identify when two sources agree, disagree, or add to each other.
I can synthesize information from different types of sources (written, visual, oral) into a coherent response.

competencies.raw: Recognize how different features, forms, and genres of texts reflect different purposes, audiences, and messages.
i_can_statements: I can identify a text's form, genre, and purpose and explain how these are connected.
I can analyze how an author's formal choices serve their communicative purpose.
I can compare how two texts in different genres address the same theme.

competencies.raw: Think critically, creatively, and reflectively to explore ideas within, between, and beyond texts.
i_can_statements: I can analyze the central ideas of a text and evaluate how effectively they're developed.
I can make connections between texts and evaluate what those connections reveal.
I can reflect on my own reading process and how it affects my interpretation.

competencies.raw: Recognize and identify the role of personal, social, and cultural contexts, values, and perspectives in texts.
i_can_statements: I can identify how an author's background and social context shape their perspective.
I can recognize values embedded in a text and evaluate whether I agree with them.
I can identify whose perspective is centered in a text and whose is absent.

competencies.raw: Recognize how language constructs personal, social, and cultural identity.
i_can_statements: I can explain how word choice reveals or shapes identity in a text.
I can analyze how language is used to include or exclude different groups.
I can identify how dominant cultural narratives are reinforced or challenged through language.

competencies.raw: Construct meaningful personal connections between self, text, and world.
i_can_statements: I can make a personal connection to a text and explain what it adds to my understanding.
I can connect a text to events or issues in the world and analyze the relationship.
I can explain how my identity shapes what I notice and how I respond to a text.

competencies.raw: Respond to text in personal, creative, and critical ways.
i_can_statements: I can write a personal response to a text, drawing on my own experience and perspective.
I can create a creative response that demonstrates deep engagement with the original text.
I can write a critical analysis of a text using specific evidence and my own interpretation.

competencies.raw: Explain how literary elements, techniques, and devices enhance and shape meaning.
i_can_statements: I can identify a literary device and explain with evidence how it creates a specific effect.
I can analyze how irony, paradox, or oxymoron creates meaning in a text.
I can compare how two authors use the same literary technique differently.

competencies.raw: Recognize an increasing range of text structures and how they contribute to meaning.
i_can_statements: I can identify the structure of a complex text and explain how it organizes meaning.
I can analyze how a non-linear or cyclical structure affects the reader's experience.
I can compare the structure of a written text with an oral or visual text on the same topic.

competencies.raw: Recognize and appreciate the role of story, narrative, and oral tradition in expressing First Peoples perspectives, values, beliefs, and points of view.
i_can_statements: I can explain how oral tradition functions as history, law, and cultural transmission in First Peoples communities.
I can describe the values or teachings embedded in a First Peoples story.
I can analyze how a First Peoples narrative structure (circular, iterative) differs from Western linear narrative.

competencies.raw: Develop awareness of the diversity within and across First Peoples societies represented in texts.
i_can_statements: I can identify the specific nation or community represented in a First Peoples text.
I can explain why it's important not to treat all First Peoples cultures as the same.
I can compare two texts from different First Peoples nations and identify similarities and differences.

competencies.raw: Recognize the influence of place in First Peoples and other Canadian texts.
i_can_statements: I can analyze how a specific place shapes the identity, values, and experiences in a text.
I can explain how land and place function differently in First Peoples texts than in many Western texts.
I can identify how setting contributes to meaning in a Canadian context.

categories.name: Create and Communicate (Writing, Speaking, Representing)
categories.raw: Create and Communicate — Writing, Speaking, Representing

competencies.raw: Exchange ideas and viewpoints to build shared understanding and extend thinking.
i_can_statements: I can participate in discussions where I both share my views and genuinely build on others' ideas.
I can paraphrase and respond respectfully to a classmate's point of view.
I can help a group arrive at a deeper understanding through collaborative dialogue.

competencies.raw: Use writing and design processes to plan, develop, and create engaging and meaningful literary and informational texts for a variety of purposes and audiences.
i_can_statements: I can plan a piece of writing with a clear sense of my purpose, audience, and key ideas.
I can draft, revise, and edit writing through multiple stages.
I can make intentional design choices (layout, headings, visuals) to suit my audience and purpose.

competencies.raw: Assess and refine texts to improve their clarity, effectiveness, and impact according to purpose, audience, and message.
i_can_statements: I can critically evaluate my own writing and identify what works and what needs improvement.
I can use peer feedback to make targeted revisions that strengthen my writing.
I can explain the choices I made in revising and how they improved the final text.

competencies.raw: Use an increasing repertoire of conventions of Canadian spelling, grammar, and punctuation.
i_can_statements: I can apply a growing range of grammar and punctuation conventions accurately.
I can recognize and correct common errors (double negatives, mixed metaphors, word misuse).
I can explain the purpose of specific conventions and use them purposefully.

competencies.raw: Use and experiment with oral storytelling processes.
i_can_statements: I can deliver a story orally with strong expression, pacing, and engagement.
I can experiment with oral techniques such as call-and-response, repetition, and dramatic pause.
I can adapt a written text for an oral storytelling context.

competencies.raw: Select and use appropriate features, forms, and genres according to audience, purpose, and message.
i_can_statements: I can justify my choice of form or genre by explaining how it serves my purpose.
I can experiment with different forms (spoken word, essay, narrative, report) for the same content.
I can analyze how professional writers make genre choices and apply that learning to my own writing.

competencies.raw: Transform ideas and information to create original texts.
i_can_statements: I can transform research or ideas into original writing that reflects my own voice and interpretation.
I can create a text in a different form than my source material while preserving the key ideas.
I can demonstrate creative ownership in all my writing — not just summarize or copy.

competencies.raw: Express an opinion and support it with credible evidence.
i_can_statements: I can clearly state my opinion or position on a topic.
I can select and use credible evidence to support my argument.
I can address counterarguments and explain why my position is still valid.

areas.name: Story/Text — Forms, Functions, and Genres
areas.raw: Narrative, exposition, report; purposes of text; literary or thematic categories such as fantasy, humour, adventure, biography.
topics: Narrative, expository, and report text forms
Purposes of text: to entertain, inform, persuade, explore, express
Genres: fantasy, humour, adventure, biography, memoir, mystery
Distinguishing literary and informational text types

areas.name: Story/Text — Literary Elements and Devices
areas.raw: Characterization, narrative structures, setting; sensory detail, figurative language, irony, paradox, oxymoron.
topics: Characterization: direct and indirect, dynamic and static
Narrative structures: linear, cyclical, iterative; narrative structures in First Peoples texts
Setting: place, time, atmosphere, and its influence on character
Imagery and sensory detail
Sound devices: alliteration, assonance, onomatopoeia
Figurative language: metaphor, simile, personification, hyperbole
Irony: dramatic, situational, and verbal
Paradox and oxymoron

areas.name: Story/Text — Visual and Graphic Texts
areas.raw: Layout, infographics, emoticons, icons, symbols, interactive visuals, hypertext, colour; illustration style (realism, cartoon, sketch, outline).
topics: Layout and design in digital and print texts
Infographics: communicating data visually
Illustration styles: realism, cartoon, sketch, outline
Icons, emoticons, and symbols as language
Hypertext and digital interactive texts
Colour theory and its use in visual communication

areas.name: Strategies and Processes
areas.raw: Reading strategies, oral language strategies, metacognitive strategies, writing processes.
topics: Reading strategies: contextual clues, word structure, visualizing, questioning, predicting, previewing, summarizing, inferring
Oral language strategies: focused listening, clarifying questions, expressing opinions, speaking with expression, taking turns
Metacognitive strategies: thinking about thinking, reflecting, goal setting, self-evaluating
Writing processes: generating ideas, planning, drafting, revising, editing

areas.name: Language Features, Structures, and Conventions
areas.raw: Features of oral language, multi-paragraphing, language change, elements of style, usage, syntax and sentence fluency, conventions, rhetorical devices, connotation and denotation.
topics: Oral language features: tone, volume, inflection, pace, gestures
Multi-paragraph writing: unity, development, coherence
Language change: how languages evolve over time (e.g., Old English to Modern English)
Elements of style: diction, figurative language, tone, inclusive language, formality
Usage: avoiding double negatives, mixed metaphors, malapropisms, word misuse
Syntax: simple, compound, and complex sentences; pronoun use; subject-verb agreement
Conventions: standard punctuation, capitalization, quoting, Canadian spelling
Rhetorical devices: parallelism, repetition, irony, humour, emotional language, direct address, rhetorical questions, allusion
Connotation and denotation: the difference between word meaning and emotional association
